name: AH-64 Apache
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The AH-64 Apache is the US Army's primary attack helicopter, armed with a 30mm M230 chain gun, AGM-114 Hellfire missiles, and 70mm Hydra rockets. Its advanced target acquisition and night vision systems make it lethal in all weather conditions.
historicalBackground: Developed in the 1970s as a replacement for the AH-1 Cobra, the Apache first flew in September 1975 and entered service in 1986. It gained worldwide fame during Operation Desert Storm in 1991, where Apache crews flew the first shots of the war against Iraqi radar sites.

name: UH-60 Black Hawk
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The UH-60 Black Hawk is the US Army's workhorse utility helicopter, capable of carrying 11 combat-equipped soldiers. It serves in assault, medevac, VIP transport, special operations, and disaster relief roles across more than 25 countries.
historicalBackground: Winner of the Army's UTTAS competition in 1976, the Black Hawk replaced the UH-1 Huey. It debuted in combat in Grenada in 1983 and became internationally known after the Battle of Mogadishu in 1993, depicted in the book and film 'Black Hawk Down'.
famousMissions: Operation Neptune Spear – Bin Laden raid (2011)

name: CH-47 Chinook
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The CH-47 Chinook is a twin-engine, tandem rotor heavy-lift helicopter capable of transporting 33–55 troops or up to 10,886 kg of cargo. Its distinctive tandem rotor eliminates the need for a tail rotor, providing exceptional stability at high altitudes.
historicalBackground: Developed in the late 1950s, the Chinook first flew in 1961 and entered US Army service in 1962. It served extensively in the Vietnam War as the primary heavy-lift helicopter. Continuously upgraded, the CH-47F remains in production more than 60 years later.

name: Mi-24 Hind
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Mi-24 Hind is a large attack and assault helicopter famous for its heavily armored fuselage and ability to carry both weapons and troops simultaneously — a combination unique among combat helicopters. NATO pilots nicknamed it the 'Devil's Chariot'.
historicalBackground: Designed in the late 1960s under Mikhail Mil, the Mi-24 entered Soviet service in 1972. It became a feared weapon during the Soviet-Afghan War (1979–1989), where its armored body proved surprisingly resilient. It has seen combat in over 30 conflicts worldwide.

name: NH90
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The NH90 is a medium-sized, twin-engine military helicopter developed by a NATO consortium. Available in a tactical transport (TTH) and naval frigate helicopter (NFH) variant, it features a full fly-by-wire control system and composite airframe.
historicalBackground: Developed in response to a 1985 NATO requirement, the NH90 is one of the largest collaborative helicopter programs in history. It entered service with Germany, France, Italy, and the Netherlands in the mid-2000s. Finland controversially cancelled its order in 2022 citing performance issues.

name: EC135 / H135
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney PW206 or Turboméca Arrius)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The EC135 is one of the world's most popular twin-engine light helicopters, widely used for emergency medical services, police, offshore, and VIP transport. Its Fenestron shrouded tail rotor makes it exceptionally quiet and safe around populated areas.
historicalBackground: Derived from the Bo 105 and BK 117 lineage, the EC135 first flew in 1994 and has been delivered to operators in more than 50 countries. It is the primary training helicopter for many military pilots and is used extensively by Germany's ADAC air rescue service.

name: SH-60 Seahawk
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor (foldable)
description: The SH-60 Seahawk is the naval variant of the Black Hawk, serving as the US Navy's primary shipborne helicopter for anti-submarine warfare, anti-ship missiles, search and rescue, and special operations. Its folding rotors allow storage on small frigate decks.
historicalBackground: Selected in 1977 to replace the SH-2 Seasprite, the Seahawk first flew in 1979 and entered service in 1984. The MH-60R 'Romeo' variant, introduced in 2005, is considered the world's most capable maritime helicopter, integrating radar, sonar, and weapons in one platform.

name: Mi-8 Hip
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Mi-8 is the world's most produced helicopter, with over 17,000 built. It serves in military, civilian, VIP, firefighting, Antarctic, and armed gunship roles. Its robust construction and simple maintenance have made it indispensable in harsh environments from the Arctic to Africa.
historicalBackground: Designed by Mikhail Mil and first flown in 1961, the Mi-8 was intended as a successor to the piston-powered Mi-4. It entered Soviet service in 1967 and became a Cold War staple, equipping the air forces of nearly every Warsaw Pact nation and dozens of developing countries.
famousMissions: UN peacekeeping in Africa and the Balkans

name: CH-53E Super Stallion
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (7 blades) + tail rotor
description: The CH-53E Super Stallion is the US military's largest and heaviest helicopter, capable of lifting 90% of all Marine Corps equipment. Its three engines and seven-blade rotor provide unmatched heavy-lift capacity for the amphibious assault mission.
historicalBackground: Developed from the twin-engine CH-53D, the three-engine Super Stallion first flew in 1974 and entered Marine Corps service in 1981. It has served as the backbone of USMC heavy lift for four decades. Its successor, the CH-53K King Stallion, began deliveries in 2018.

name: AW101 Merlin
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW101 Merlin is a large, three-engine helicopter used in naval anti-submarine warfare, VIP transport, and combat search and rescue. The RAF and Royal Navy operate it as the Merlin HC3 and HM2 respectively. It serves as Marine One alternative for the Italian president.
historicalBackground: A joint development between Agusta and Westland beginning in the 1980s, the EH101 (later AW101) first flew in 1987. After a lengthy development, it entered service with the Royal Navy in 1999. Canada controversially cancelled then reinstated its Cyclone variant order.
famousMissions: Royal Navy ASW patrols – North Atlantic

name: Ka-52 Alligator
rotorConfig: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors
description: The Ka-52 is Russia's frontline attack helicopter, unique for its side-by-side cockpit seating and coaxial rotor system requiring no tail rotor. Armed with a 30mm cannon, Vikhr anti-tank missiles, and unguided rockets, it serves as both scout and attack platform.
historicalBackground: Developed from the Ka-50 Black Shark in the 1990s, the Ka-52 first flew in 1996 but budget constraints delayed service entry until 2011. The coaxial design is a hallmark of Kamov, eliminating tail rotor torque and improving agility. Egypt became the first export customer in 2015.

name: Tiger HAD
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Tiger is Europe's primary attack helicopter, built for Germany, France, Spain, and Australia. The HAD (Hélicoptère d'Appui Destruction) variant carries the Mistral air-to-air missile and Hellfire, making it highly capable in both anti-armour and air combat roles.
historicalBackground: A Franco-German program launched in 1984, the Tiger overcame Cold War-era procurement disputes to first fly in 1991. Australia acquired the ARH variant in 2004. France deployed Tigers to Afghanistan in 2009 — their first combat operation — and later to Mali and Libya.

name: AW139
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW139 is a medium-large twin-engine helicopter highly regarded in offshore oil, SAR, law enforcement, EMS, and VIP transport. With 15 passengers capacity and exceptional range, it has become one of the most commercially successful helicopters of the 21st century.
historicalBackground: Developed jointly by AgustaWestland and Bell (as the AB139) before Leonardo took full control, the AW139 first flew in 2001. The US Presidential helicopter fleet (VH-92, based on S-92) beat out an AW101 variant. Over 1,000 AW139s are in service worldwide.

name: Robinson R44 Raven
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Robinson R44 is the world's best-selling general aviation helicopter, widely used for private ownership, flight training, news gathering, and light utility work. Its affordable purchase price and operating costs have democratized helicopter ownership worldwide.
historicalBackground: Designed by Frank Robinson following the R22, the R44 first flew in 1990 and entered production in 1993. It quickly became the top-selling helicopter in the world. The R44 Cadet (2-seat trainer) and Newscopter variants have expanded its market reach significantly.

name: AH-1Z Viper
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The AH-1Z Viper is the US Marine Corps' primary attack helicopter, a radical upgrade of the Vietnam-era AH-1 Cobra with a new four-blade composite rotor, twin-engine power, and a targeting system equal to the Apache's. It can also engage air targets with Sidewinder missiles.
historicalBackground: The AH-1Z emerged from the H-1 upgrade program that modernised the USMC's Cobra fleet. Sharing 84% common parts with the UH-1Y, it simplified Marine logistics. It first flew in 2000, entered service in 2010, and saw combat in Afghanistan the same year.

name: H225M Caracal
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The H225M Caracal is a military transport and special operations helicopter used for combat SAR, special forces insertion, and long-range maritime patrol. Equipped with in-flight refueling capability, it can operate deep behind enemy lines with extended range.
historicalBackground: Derived from the civilian EC225 Super Puma, the H225M entered French service in 2005. It gained notoriety in 2016 when the civilian variant was grounded after two North Sea fatal crashes due to a gearbox failure, but the military variant retained a clean safety record.

name: Mi-26 Halo
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (8 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-26 Halo is the world's largest and most powerful helicopter ever to have entered serial production. With a payload exceeding 20 tonnes and a cargo hold the size of a C-130 Hercules, it has no equivalent in Western aviation.
historicalBackground: Developed to replace the Mi-6, the Mi-26 first flew in 1977 and entered Soviet military service in 1983. In 2002, one was used to extract a trapped Chinook from the mountains of Shankh, Afghanistan, by slinging it externally — the heaviest helicopter sling load in history.
famousMissions: Extraction of downed CH-47 in Afghanistan (2002)

name: OH-58D Kiowa Warrior
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The OH-58D Kiowa Warrior is an armed reconnaissance helicopter equipped with a mast-mounted sight ball above the rotor, allowing it to observe while hidden behind terrain. Armed with Hellfire missiles, Hydra rockets, and machine guns, it flew alongside Apaches as scout-attack teams.
historicalBackground: Selected in 1968 to replace the OH-13 Sioux, the OH-58 flew in Vietnam. The D model with mast-mounted sight was introduced in the 1980s. The Kiowa Warrior saw extensive combat in the Gulf War, Somalia, Bosnia, Iraq, and Afghanistan before the US Army retired it in 2014.

name: Bo 105
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid hingeless main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bo 105 was revolutionary as the world's first helicopter with a rigid rotor system, enabling aerobatic maneuvers such as inverted flight and loops. Small yet powerful, it served as an anti-tank helicopter carrying HOT missiles and as an EMS aircraft across 40 countries.
historicalBackground: Designed by Ludwig Bölkow at MBB, the Bo 105 first flew in 1967 and became one of Germany's most significant aviation exports. Germany's military 'VBH' and 'PAH-1' variants served through the Cold War. Production ended in 2001 after more than 1,400 were built.
famousMissions: German Army Cold War anti-tank patrols

name: Sikorsky S-76
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney PW210S or Turbomeca Arriel 2S2)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-76 is a sleek twin-engine helicopter widely used for offshore oil platform transport, VIP and executive transport, EMS, and search and rescue. Kobe Bryant and seven others were killed in an S-76B crash in California in January 2020.
historicalBackground: Sikorsky developed the S-76 'Spirit' as its first purpose-designed commercial helicopter, debuting in 1977. Queen Elizabeth II used the S-76 for British Royal Family transport for decades. The US Army operates a variant as the UH-72 Lakota trainer.

name: AS350 / H125 Écureuil
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The AS350 Écureuil (Squirrel) is one of the world's most popular single-engine turbine helicopters, prized for its high-altitude performance and versatility. In 2005, it became the first helicopter to land on the summit of Mount Everest.
historicalBackground: Developed by Aérospatiale and first flown in 1974, the Écureuil was designed for high-hot-high performance. A US Army variant (OH-58) competition was lost to Bell but the AS350 found huge commercial success. Over 4,800 have been built in France and Brazil.

name: CH-53K King Stallion
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (7 composite blades) + tail rotor
description: The CH-53K King Stallion is the world's most powerful helicopter, with three times the lift capacity of its predecessor. Designed for the USMC, it can carry 27 tonnes on its external hook, enabling it to lift virtually any Marine Corps vehicle or artillery piece.
historicalBackground: Program begun in 2006 as a replacement for the aging CH-53E, the King Stallion first flew in 2015. With advanced fly-by-wire, composite rotor blades, and new engines producing 7,500 shaft horsepower each, it represents a generation leap over all predecessors. IOC achieved in 2022.

name: Bell 206 JetRanger
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 206 JetRanger is an iconic light turbine helicopter that has appeared in more films and television shows than any other helicopter in history. With over 7,300 built, it is used worldwide for pilot training, police patrol, news gathering, offshore support, and private transport.
historicalBackground: Originally designed as the OH-4A for the Army's LOH competition (which it lost to the Hughes OH-6), Bell refined it into the commercial JetRanger in 1966. The US Army subsequently ordered it as the OH-58 Kiowa. The JetRanger starred in Magnum P.I., Airwolf, and Blue Thunder.

name: AW109 Power
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW109 is Italy's premier twin-engine light helicopter, known for its sleek aerodynamic design and high performance. It serves as Belgium's army scout helicopter, and the Italian Army flies an armed version with rockets and anti-tank missiles.
historicalBackground: Agusta's first twin-engine helicopter design, the A109 first flew in 1971 after a lengthy development. The A109 achieved worldwide fame when one was used by the Belgian Air Component to rush a donor heart to a transplant. The Belgian military operates armed A109s in the scout role.

name: Westland Lynx
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Rolls-Royce Gem or LHTEC CTS800)
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid hingeless main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Lynx is a compact British helicopter that held the world absolute speed record for helicopters at 400.87 km/h from 1986 to 2013. Its semi-rigid rotor gives it outstanding agility. It serves in the naval ASW/ASuW and army utility roles across 11 nations.
historicalBackground: Developed jointly by Westland and Aérospatiale in the early 1970s, the Lynx first flew in 1971. It entered Royal Navy service in 1976 and British Army service in 1978. During the Falklands War, a Royal Navy Lynx sank an Argentine patrol boat with Sea Skua missiles.
famousMissions: Falklands War – ARA Alferez Sobral sinking (1982)

name: Bell UH-1 Iroquois (Huey)
engineType: Single turboshaft (Lycoming T53 or Bell T5309)
rotorConfig: Two-blade semi-rigid rotor + tail rotor
description: The UH-1 Huey is the defining helicopter of the Vietnam War era, its distinctive two-blade 'wop-wop' sound instantly recognisable. Over 16,000 were built, making it the most produced military helicopter in Western history. It served as medevac, troop carrier, and gunship.
historicalBackground: Winner of the Army's 1955 utility helicopter competition, the HU-1 (later UH-1) first flew in 1956 and entered service in 1959. During the Vietnam War, the Huey became the symbol of American military intervention. The Bell 204/205/212 family continues in production today.
famousMissions: Vietnam War medevac and air assault (1962–1975)

name: Ka-27 Helix
rotorConfig: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors
description: The Ka-27 is Russia's primary naval shipborne helicopter, designed for anti-submarine warfare and search and rescue. Its coaxial rotor design eliminates the tail rotor entirely, greatly reducing the length required on ship decks and improving hover stability in gusting sea winds.
historicalBackground: Developed in the 1970s to replace the Ka-25, the Ka-27 first flew in 1973 and entered Soviet Navy service in 1982. Export versions (Ka-28) serve in China, India, Vietnam, and Yugoslavia. The coaxial design proved crucial for operation from small frigate and destroyer decks.
famousMissions: Soviet/Russian naval ASW patrols – North Atlantic & Pacific

name: EC225 / H225 Super Puma
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The EC225 Super Puma is the dominant long-range offshore helicopter for oil and gas operations, capable of flying up to 200 miles offshore with a full load of 19 passengers. It also serves as a VIP transport, carrying heads of state including the French President.
historicalBackground: Successor to the AS332 Super Puma, the EC225 entered service in 2004 and quickly became the preferred helicopter for North Sea offshore operations. After two fatal crashes in Norway in 2016 linked to a gearbox fault, the type was grounded for 14 months before returning to service.
famousMissions: North Sea oil platform support operations

name: CAIC Z-10
engineType: Twin turboshaft (WZ-9 or Pratt & Whitney PT6C)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Z-10 is China's first dedicated attack helicopter, comparable in concept to the Apache. Armed with a 23mm cannon, HJ-10 anti-tank missiles, and TY-90 air-to-air missiles, it gives the PLA Army Aviation a modern anti-armour and escort capability.
historicalBackground: Development began in the late 1990s with secret design assistance from Kamov engineers. The Z-10 first flew in 2003 and entered PLA service around 2012. It was revealed publicly at the 2012 Zhuhai airshow. It has been deployed to Tibet for high-altitude operations.
famousMissions: PLA Army Aviation exercises in Tibet

name: H145 / EC145
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The H145 is a versatile twin-engine light helicopter widely used in EMS, law enforcement, SAR, and as a military light transport. The US Army flies it as the UH-72A Lakota for training and national guard duties. Its five-blade composite rotor and Fenestron make it very quiet.
historicalBackground: A collaborative development between Eurocopter Germany and Japan's Kawasaki (as the BK117 successor), the EC145 first flew in 1999. The US Army selected it over the Bell 412EP and Sikorsky H-92 for the Lakota contract in 2006, ordering over 300 aircraft.
famousMissions: US Army National Guard border security operations

name: VH-92A
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The VH-92A is the latest Marine One presidential helicopter, replacing the ageing VH-3D Sea King. Built on the commercial S-92 platform with extensive secure communication systems, armored cabin, and countermeasures, it is the most protected helicopter in the world.
historicalBackground: A lengthy and troubled procurement to replace the VH-3 Sea King began in 2002 with the VH-71 Kestrel program (a modified AW101), which was cancelled in 2009 after cost overruns. The VH-92 program was restarted in 2014 with Sikorsky's S-92; IOC was declared in December 2020.
famousMissions: Transport of US President from White House to Air Force One (daily)

name: Mi-171Sh
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Mi-171Sh is the combat-optimized assault variant of the Mi-8/Mi-17 family, featuring enhanced armor, stub wings for weapons, a rear ramp for rapid troop deployment, and upgraded engines. It represents the modern evolution of the world's most-produced helicopter.
historicalBackground: A product of the post-Soviet upgrade of the Mi-8/17 family, the Mi-171Sh was developed in the 1990s for the export market. Its combination of Mi-8 reliability with Mi-24-like weapons capability at an affordable price made it one of Russia's most successful defense exports of the 2000s and 2010s.
famousMissions: Czech and Slovak Republic peacekeeping operations in Afghanistan

name: AW159 Wildcat
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid hingeless main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW159 Wildcat is the Royal Navy and Army Air Corps' replacement for the Lynx, modernised with new engines, glass cockpit, advanced radar, and weapons fit. The naval variant carries Stingray torpedoes and Sea Venom missiles for anti-submarine and anti-ship operations.
historicalBackground: Procured under the UK's Future Lynx program, the Wildcat first flew in 2009 and entered service in 2015. South Korea became the first export customer in 2013. During the development phase, AgustaWestland was acquired by Leonardo. The Wildcat uses 90% new parts versus the old Lynx.
famousMissions: Royal Navy frigate deployments – Gulf of Aden counter-piracy

name: H160
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 biplane blades) + Fenestron
description: The H160 is the latest generation medium helicopter from Airbus, featuring innovative biplane rotor blades (Blue Edge) that reduce noise dramatically, a tilted Fenestron, and extensive composite airframe. It replaces the Dauphin family in offshore and EMS roles.
historicalBackground: Launched at Heli-Expo 2015 when the prototype first flew, the H160 represents Airbus's largest civil helicopter investment in two decades. The French military selected a military variant, the H160M Guépard, in 2020 to replace the Dauphin and Alouette III across the Army, Navy, and Air Force.

name: Sikorsky S-92
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-92 is the benchmark medium-large twin-engine helicopter for offshore oil and gas transport and search and rescue, able to carry 19 passengers over 200 nautical miles. The VH-92 presidential variant serves as Marine One.
historicalBackground: Developed in the 1990s with international partners from Japan, China, and Brazil, the S-92 first flew in 1998. It entered service in 2004. Cougar Helicopters Flight 491 crashed into the Atlantic off Newfoundland in 2009, killing 17, leading to major safety improvements to the oil filter system.
famousMissions: North Sea offshore oil platform operations

name: HAIG Z-20
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Z-20 is China's domestically developed medium utility helicopter, broadly comparable to the UH-60 Black Hawk, featuring a five-blade composite rotor, tail rotor, and fly-by-wire controls. It entered service with the PLA Army, Navy, and Air Force from 2019.
historicalBackground: Development followed China's acquisition of 24 S-70C Black Hawks in 1984, many of which were reversed-engineered. The Z-20 first appeared publicly in 2013, entered service c.2019, and is steadily replacing the imported S-70 in Chinese military service. A naval variant is carrier-deployable.

name: Aérospatiale SA 341 Gazelle
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + Fenestron shrouded tail rotor
description: The Gazelle is a sleek, elegant French light helicopter notable for its Fenestron shrouded tail rotor — a feature that later became Airbus Helicopters' trademark. Fast and agile, it serves as a scout, armed with HOT anti-tank missiles in French and British service.
historicalBackground: A Franco-British collaborative design, the Gazelle first flew in 1967 as part of the same agreement that produced the Lynx and Puma. It entered French service in 1973. Syrian Arab Air Force Gazelles saw heavy action in the 1982 Lebanon War, firing HOT missiles against Israeli tanks.
famousMissions: 1982 Lebanon War – Bekaa Valley anti-tank operations

name: HH-60G Pave Hawk
role: Combat Search and Rescue / Special Ops
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The HH-60G Pave Hawk is the USAF's Combat Search and Rescue helicopter, equipped with terrain-following radar, FLIR, GPS, in-flight refueling probe, and comprehensive ECM. It is designed to penetrate enemy airspace at night and low altitude to recover downed aircrew.
historicalBackground: Modified from the UH-60 Black Hawk specifically for CSAR and special operations in the 1980s, the Pave Hawk first deployed to combat in Panama in 1989. It gained fame during the Gulf War and was the helicopter used in the failed Iranian hostage rescue precursor planning. The HH-60W Jolly Green II succeeds it.

name: MQ-8 Fire Scout
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The MQ-8 Fire Scout is the US Navy's ship-launched unmanned helicopter, capable of autonomous takeoff and landing on moving vessels. It provides persistent ISR (intelligence, surveillance, reconnaissance) and can be armed with laser-guided rockets for strike missions.
historicalBackground: Developed from Schweizer's civil helicopter by Northrop Grumman, the Fire Scout first flew in 2000. The B variant (based on the Bell 407 airframe) carries more payload and has greater range. It deployed to Afghanistan in 2011, and has supported anti-narcotics operations in the Caribbean.

name: PZL W-3 Sokół
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The PZL W-3 Sokół (Falcon) is Poland's only domestically designed and produced helicopter, unique in Eastern Europe as a completely indigenous design — not a Soviet licence copy. It serves the Polish Army, Border Guard, Navy, Police, and SAR services.
historicalBackground: Development began in the 1970s, remarkable for a communist-era country producing its own helicopter outside Soviet licensed designs. The Sokół first flew in 1979 and entered service in 1985. It was exported to Myanmar and South Korea. An armed variant, the W-3W Głuszec, carries anti-tank missiles.
famousMissions: Polish Navy Baltic Sea SAR operations

name: AW129 Mangusta
engineType: Twin turboshaft (LHTEC T800 or Rolls-Royce Gem)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The AW129 Mangusta (Mongoose) is Europe's first dedicated attack helicopter, developed exclusively for Italy. Armed with a 20mm cannon, Hellfire missiles, Stinger air-to-air missiles, and rockets, it participated in multiple NATO operations in the Balkans and Afghanistan.
historicalBackground: Development began in 1978 as Italy sought a home-grown attack helicopter. The Mangusta first flew in 1983 and entered Italian Army service in 1990. It saw its first combat in Somalia in 1993 and later served in Albania, Bosnia, and Afghanistan with Italian and Turkish variants.

name: TAI T129 ATAK
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The T129 ATAK is Turkey's license-built and heavily modified attack helicopter derived from the Italian A129 Mangusta. Fitted with indigenous Turkish weapons including the UMTAS long-range anti-tank missile and Cirit laser-guided rocket, it has seen extensive combat in anti-PKK operations.
historicalBackground: Turkey selected the ATAK in 2007 to meet its attack helicopter needs, with TAI license-building and substantially modifying the Italian A129. Engine export restrictions (US-origin LHTEC T800) blocked exports to Pakistan, driving Turkey to develop the indigenous TEI TS1400 engine. Philippines ordered 6 in 2021.

name: MD Explorer MD902
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + NOTAR (no tail rotor)
description: The MD Explorer is a twin-engine light helicopter famous for its NOTAR (No Tail Rotor) anti-torque system, which uses a fan-driven air jet to counteract main rotor torque instead of a conventional tail rotor. This eliminates the most dangerous component of a helicopter for ground crews.
historicalBackground: Developed by McDonnell Douglas Helicopters in the late 1980s, the Explorer first flew in 1992 and entered production in 1994. After Boeing acquired MDH and subsequently sold it to MD, the Explorer found a niche in EMS and law enforcement. The UK Air Ambulance fleet operated them extensively.

name: Ka-50 Black Shark
rotorConfig: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors
description: The Ka-50 Black Shark is the world's only single-seat operational attack helicopter, featuring a unique ejection seat system where the rotor blades are explosively jettisoned before the seat fires. Its coaxial rotors and sophisticated avionics allow one pilot to manage weapons and flight simultaneously.
historicalBackground: Winning a bitterly contested Soviet competition against the Mi-28 in 1984, the Ka-50 first flew in June 1982 and entered limited Russian service in 1995. Budget constraints meant only a handful were built. It saw combat in Chechnya in 2001. The two-seat Ka-52 Alligator became the preferred production variant.

name: Bell 412
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6T-3BE Twin Pac)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 composite blades) + tail rotor
description: The Bell 412 is a stretched, four-blade composite rotor development of the Bell 212, widely used by military and civilian operators worldwide. It offers improved performance over its predecessor with a quieter, more efficient rotor system.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1979, the Bell 412 replaced the twin-blade 212 with a composite four-blade rotor developed by Bell Canada. The military variant, UH-1N Twin Huey successor, serves in over 30 countries. The EP variant (Enhanced Performance) introduced in 1990 became the definitive production model.

name: Mi-28N Night Hunter
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-28N Night Hunter is Russia's dedicated attack helicopter, competing with the Ka-50/52. Equipped with a mast-mounted radar, FLIR, and night vision systems, it carries the 9M120 Ataka anti-tank missile and a 30mm 2A42 cannon. It is a pure attack platform with no troop transport capability.
historicalBackground: Losing the Soviet attack helicopter competition to the Ka-50 in 1984, Mil continued developing the Mi-28 and eventually secured Russian Army orders after the USSR's dissolution. The N (Nochnoj – Night) variant with mast radar entered service in 2009. It saw combat in Syria from 2015.

name: EC120B Colibri
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The EC120 Colibri is a five-seat single-engine light helicopter built as an international collaboration between France, China, and Singapore. Its Fenestron tail rotor and turbine engine provide quiet, efficient flight ideal for flight training schools and private operators.
historicalBackground: A joint venture between Eurocopter, CATIC of China, and Singaporean Technologies Aerospace, the EC120 first flew in 1995 and entered production in 1997. It became popular with flight training organizations worldwide and serves as a primary pilot training helicopter in France and China.

name: HH-65 Dolphin
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The HH-65 Dolphin is the US Coast Guard's primary short-range recovery helicopter, operated alongside the larger MH-60T Jayhawk. It is known for its distinctive Fenestron tail rotor and vivid USCG livery. The helicopter is also used for port security and counter-narcotics.
historicalBackground: Selected by the USCG in 1979 in preference to the Sikorsky S-76, the HH-65 entered service in 1985. A prolonged engine replacement program in the 2000s swapped the original unreliable Lycoming LTS101 engines for Turbomeca Arriel turbines. Over 100 serve with the USCG today.

name: UH-72 Lakota
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The UH-72 Lakota is the US Army's light utility helicopter, serving the Army National Guard for medevac, reconnaissance, C2, and pilot training. Based on the Eurocopter EC145, it replaced the aging UH-1 and OH-58 fleets in stateside National Guard units.
historicalBackground: The US Army selected the EC145 over Bell 412EP and Sikorsky H-92 in 2006, ordering 322 aircraft. Deliveries ran from 2007 to 2013. As the only non-American-designed helicopter in the Army's current fleet, it sparked political controversy but offered significant cost savings.
famousMissions: US Army National Guard domestic emergency response

name: CH-46 Sea Knight
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The CH-46 Sea Knight served the US Marine Corps for over 45 years as its primary medium-lift assault helicopter, replaced only by the V-22 Osprey in 2015. Its tandem rotor design provides stability and a wide rear ramp for rapid troop and cargo loading.
historicalBackground: Ordered by the Marines in 1960 following the Chinook's success, the Sea Knight first flew in 1958 and entered USMC service in 1964. It served throughout Vietnam, the Gulf War, Somalia, Iraq, and Afghanistan before retirement in 2015. The Canadian CH-113 Labrador variant served until 2004.

name: Sikorsky VH-3D Sea King
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades, foldable) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky Sea King is best known as Marine One when carrying the US President, a role it held from President Kennedy to President Trump. Its amphibious hull, folding rotors for shipboard storage, and all-weather capability made it the gold standard for naval and VIP helicopters.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1959 to meet a Navy ASW requirement, the SH-3 Sea King entered naval service in 1961. The VH-3 variant became Marine One in 1962. Queen Elizabeth II used a British Westland Sea King. The type served in over 12 nations and was finally retired by the US Marines in 2023 after 61 years.

name: Sikorsky S-58 / CH-34 Choctaw
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The S-58 Choctaw was the dominant Western military helicopter of the late 1950s and 1960s, serving all US armed services simultaneously. The nose-mounted radial engine gave it a distinctive appearance. It bridged the gap between piston-powered and turbine helicopter eras.
historicalBackground: Sikorsky's answer to the Navy's demand for a dedicated ASW helicopter, the S-58 first flew in 1954. It served in Algeria, Vietnam, and throughout NATO. France used it extensively during the Algerian War. Westland built it under license as the Wessex with turbine engines, serving the Royal Navy until 2004.
famousMissions: US Navy Cold War ASW patrols

name: Guimbal Cabri G2
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The Cabri G2 is a modern two-seat training helicopter with a Fenestron tail rotor and energy-absorbing landing gear, designed with exceptional safety features. Its crashworthy design and enhanced structural integrity make it a favourite replacement for the Robinson R22 in flight schools.
historicalBackground: Designed by Bruno Guimbal of Eurocopter, the Cabri G2 first flew in 2005 and received EASA certification in 2007. It incorporated lessons learned from decades of helicopter accident analysis, making it the safest light piston helicopter on the market. Over 300 serve worldwide in flight academies.

name: Kaman K-MAX
rotorConfig: Intermeshing synchropter (2 rotors, no tail rotor)
description: The K-MAX is a specialized aerial crane helicopter using an unusual intermeshing rotor (synchropter) design, giving it a lift-to-weight ratio superior to conventional helicopters. USMC used an unmanned K-MAX to deliver cargo in Afghanistan, the first operational unmanned helicopter logistics mission.
historicalBackground: Developed by Kaman based on their intermeshing rotor technology from the 1940s, the K-MAX first flew in 1991. The US Marine Corps deployed two unmanned K-MAX aircraft in Afghanistan from 2011 to 2014, successfully delivering 4.5 million pounds of cargo while keeping pilots out of danger.

name: Robinson R22 Beta II
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade teetering rotor + tail rotor
description: The Robinson R22 is the world's most produced piston helicopter and the most common primary training helicopter globally. Its simple, lightweight design and low operating costs have trained more helicopter pilots than any other type in history.
historicalBackground: Designed by Frank Robinson after leaving Hughes, the R22 first flew in 1975 and entered production in 1979. It ignited controversy due to early accidents related to low-G flight mast bumping, leading to pilot training improvements. The Beta II (1995) improved the engine and safety features.

name: Bell Boeing V-22 Osprey
rotorConfig: Tiltrotor (tilts 97° from vertical to horizontal)
description: The V-22 Osprey is a tiltrotor aircraft that takes off and lands like a helicopter but cruises at turboprop aircraft speeds and altitudes. It provides the US military with unmatched range and speed for troop transport and special operations, though at considerable cost and complexity.
historicalBackground: Development began in 1981 after tragic failures of Operation Eagle Claw. The Osprey suffered four fatal crashes during development, killing 30 people, and was nearly cancelled multiple times. It entered USMC service in 2007 and deployed to Iraq that year. Japan ordered the V-22 in 2015.
famousMissions: Libya NEO – evacuation of US citizens (2011)

name: Schweizer 300C / Hughes 300
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid three-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Hughes/Schweizer 300 is one of the longest-produced piston helicopters in history, built continuously from 1956 to 2011. Its three-seat capacity, simple mechanics, and low cost made it the primary training helicopter for countless military and civilian pilot programs.
historicalBackground: Designed by Hughes Tool Company's aircraft division in 1956, the TH-55 Osage trained 90% of US Army pilots during the Vietnam era, over 35,000 pilots in total. Schweizer acquired the design from Hughes in 1983 and produced it until 2011. The 300C featured a more powerful engine.
famousMissions: TH-55 Osage – US Army Vietnam-era pilot training (1964–1988)

name: AW169
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney PW210A or GE H75)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW169 fills the gap between the light AW109 and medium AW139, offering ten-passenger capacity with twin-engine safety. It is gaining traction in EMS, law enforcement, and as a light military multirole platform with several nations procuring it for border patrol and SAR.
historicalBackground: Launched in 2010 and first flown in 2012, the AW169 received EASA certification in 2015. The UK selected the AW169 as its Future Light Helicopter to replace the AS350 Squirrel and Bell 212 for British Army and RAF training. Thailand and Italy's Carabinieri are operators.
famousMissions: UK Army Air Corps – Future Light Helicopter (from 2026)

name: AS565 Panther
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The AS565 Panther is the military variant of the Dauphin family, deployed on frigates and corvettes for anti-submarine warfare, anti-surface operations, and SAR. Lightweight and compact, it fits on smaller warship decks where the NH90 cannot operate.
historicalBackground: Developed from the AS365 Dauphin in the early 1980s, the Panther entered French naval service in 1993. The US Coast Guard's HH-65 Dolphin uses the same Dauphin airframe. Israel's Navy Helicopter Squadron operates armed Panthers with Spike missiles for anti-ship missions.
famousMissions: French Navy Indian Ocean ASW patrols

name: Piasecki H-25 / HUP Retriever
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The Piasecki HUP Retriever was the US Navy's first operational tandem-rotor helicopter, pioneering the configuration later perfected by the Chinook. It served as a plane guard and rescue helicopter on aircraft carriers, rescuing pilots from the water after carrier landing accidents.
historicalBackground: Frank Piasecki's second tandem-rotor design, the HUP first flew in 1948 and entered USN service in 1949. It served on carriers during the Korean War. Canada and France also operated it. It pioneered the tandem rotor configuration's advantages for shipboard operations where space is limited.
famousMissions: Korean War carrier plane guard operations
US Navy carrier deck accident rescues

name: MD 530G / AH-6 Little Bird
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AH-6 Little Bird is the US Army's special operations light attack helicopter, operated by the 160th SOAR 'Night Stalkers'. Armed with miniguns and rocket pods, it is used for direct action, escort of MH-60 and MH-47 missions, and urban combat due to its small size and agility.
historicalBackground: Based on the Hughes OH-6 Cayuse that won the 1966 LOH competition, the Little Bird was adopted for special operations in the 1980s. The 160th SOAR used AH-6s in the 1983 Grenada invasion, the 1989 Panama invasion, and most famously in the 1993 Battle of Mogadishu, depicted in 'Black Hawk Down'.

name: Aérospatiale SA 330 Puma
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The SA 330 Puma is a rugged medium transport helicopter developed for the French Army, capable of all-weather day and night operations. Its roomy cabin accommodates 16 troops or 3,200 kg of cargo. The Puma family evolved into the Super Puma, one of the world's most successful helicopter lines.
historicalBackground: Designed in response to a 1962 French Army requirement, the Puma first flew in 1965 and entered service in 1969. It was part of the Anglo-French helicopter agreement alongside the Gazelle and Lynx. The Royal Air Force operated the Puma HC1 until 2015. South Africa's Atlas built it under licence as the Oryx.

name: Aérospatiale SA 316 Alouette III
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Alouette III is a legendary French light helicopter renowned for its exceptional high-altitude performance. In 1960 it set the world helicopter altitude record at 12,440 m (40,814 ft). It served in over 70 countries and participated in some of the most dramatic mountain rescues in history.
historicalBackground: Developed from the Alouette II, the III first flew in 1959 with a more powerful engine and enclosed cabin. Switzerland's Air Rescue used it for decades in the Alps. The Indian Air Force used Alouette IIIs in the 1971 war, and the South African Air Force flew them in Angola. India built over 300 under licence as the HAL Cheetah.
famousMissions: Indian Air Force 1971 Bangladesh Liberation War

name: Piasecki CH-21 Shawnee
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The CH-21 Shawnee, nicknamed the 'Flying Banana' for its distinctive curved fuselage, was the US Army's primary transport helicopter in the early Cold War and the first American military helicopter to see significant action in Vietnam. It introduced the US military to large-scale helicopter assault tactics.
historicalBackground: Developed from the HRP Rescuer, the CH-21 first flew in 1950 and entered Army service in 1953. It was deployed to Vietnam in 1961 as part of Operation Chopper, the first combat use of US Army helicopters in Vietnam. By the mid-1960s it was replaced by the UH-1 Huey.
famousMissions: Operation Chopper – Vietnam (1961) first US helicopter combat assault

name: Enstrom F28 / 280 Shark
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Enstrom F28 is a durable three-seat piston helicopter produced since 1965 in Menominee, Michigan, making it one of the longest continuously produced helicopters in America. Known for its robust construction and reliability, it serves flight schools, police, and private owners.
historicalBackground: Designed by Rudy Enstrom and first flown in 1962, the F28 received FAA certification in 1965. The 280 Shark added aerodynamic fairings and a more powerful engine. The company changed ownership multiple times, including a period of Chinese ownership, before returning to US control. Over 1,000 have been produced.

name: HAL Dhruv (ALH)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 composite blades) + tail rotor
description: The HAL Dhruv (Advanced Light Helicopter) is India's first domestically developed helicopter, serving the Indian Army, Navy, Air Force, and Coast Guard. The armed attack variant, ALH-WSI (Rudra), carries 70mm rockets, 20mm cannon, and anti-tank missiles.
historicalBackground: Development began in 1984 with German MBB assistance, but technology transfer restrictions led India to develop increasingly indigenous components. First flown in 1992, the Dhruv entered service in 2002. It set an altitude record in 2017 when it landed on a Himalayan glacier at 6,800 m. Ecuador and Nepal have operated exports.
famousMissions: Indian Army Siachen Glacier high-altitude operations
Indian Navy anti-piracy – Gulf of Aden

name: Kamov Ka-226T
rotorConfig: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors
description: The Ka-226T is a compact coaxial light helicopter with a modular fuselage allowing rapid role change between passenger, cargo, EMS, and surveillance configurations. Its coaxial design gives it excellent high-altitude performance, making it ideal for mountain operations.
historicalBackground: Developed in the 1990s as an improved Ka-26, the Ka-226 first flew in 1997. India and Russia signed a government-to-government deal in 2015 to produce 200 Ka-226Ts in India under HAL licence, intended to replace ageing Cheetah and Chetak helicopters. Deliveries have been delayed.
famousMissions: Russian police and EMERCOM emergency operations

name: AW109 Trekker
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW109 Trekker is a single-engine derivative of the twin-engine AW109, designed for markets where twin-engine certification is not required. Its lower operating costs while retaining the AW109's glass cockpit and performance make it attractive for corporate, EMS, and government operators.
historicalBackground: Launched at the 2014 Heli-Expo and first flown the same year, the Trekker received certification in 2016. Belgium's civil protection service, Italian police, and various private operators have adopted it. It represents Leonardo's strategy to broaden the AW109 family's market reach.

name: Mi-17V-5
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Mi-17V-5 is the latest production military transport variant of the Mi-8/17 family, featuring a rear loading ramp, upgraded VK-2500 engines, sand/dust filters, ballistic protection, and advanced avionics. It is Russia's most capable military transport helicopter currently in production.
historicalBackground: The V-5 designation denotes the fifth generation of Mi-17 improvements, incorporating lessons from Afghanistan and Chechnya. The US military controversially purchased 63 Mi-17V-5s for the Afghan National Air Force between 2011 and 2014 due to Afghan crew familiarity with Russian types before switching to UH-60s.

name: Bell ARH-70 Arapaho
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell ARH-70 was the US Army's intended replacement for the OH-58D Kiowa Warrior, based on the commercial Bell 407. The program was cancelled in 2008 due to cost overruns and schedule delays, leaving the US Army without a replacement reconnaissance helicopter.
historicalBackground: Awarded to Bell in 2005 after an accelerated competition, the ARH-70 program quickly encountered major cost overruns — growing from $1.3B to $2.5B for 368 aircraft. The Army cancelled it in 2008, leaving Kiowa Warriors to soldier on until 2014 when they were retired without a replacement, creating a scout helicopter capability gap.

name: H215M Super Puma
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The H215M Super Puma is the military transport variant of the Puma/Super Puma family, used for troop transport, SAR, VIP, and utility roles. Less capable than the H225M Caracal in special operations, it fills a medium transport role for air forces requiring a cost-effective Puma family successor.
historicalBackground: The AS332 Super Puma first flew in 1978 as a twin-engine development of the SA 330. The military AS532 Cougar and later H215M designation serve air forces across Europe, Asia, and the Middle East. Thailand, Romania, and Spain operate significant fleets.
famousMissions: Spanish Air Force SAR – Mediterranean and Canary Islands

name: Sikorsky R-4 Hoverfly
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky R-4 was the world's first mass-produced helicopter and the first helicopter used operationally by any military. Only 131 were built, but they proved the helicopter's military utility in rescue, observation, and liaison roles that would define helicopter operations for generations.
historicalBackground: Growing from Igor Sikorsky's VS-300 of 1939, the R-4 became the first helicopter to enter production in 1942. It saw the world's first wartime helicopter rescue in April 1944 in Burma, when an R-4 rescued four survivors behind Japanese lines. It served the USAAF, USN, USCG, and British armed forces.
famousMissions: North Sea ASW patrols (Royal Navy trials)
USCG first helicopter rescue at sea (1945)

name: Harbin WZ-9 / Z-9
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + Fenestron tail rotor
description: The Z-9 is a Chinese licence-built and subsequently indigenous development of the Eurocopter AS365 Dauphin. It serves in utility, naval, attack, and VIP roles across the PLA Army, Navy, and Air Force. The WZ-9 armed variant carries HJ-8 anti-tank missiles and PL-90 air-to-air missiles.
historicalBackground: China purchased a licence to produce the AS365N Dauphin in 1980, with the first Chinese-assembled Z-9 flying in 1981. China progressively indigenised components, replacing French engines and systems. By the 2000s the Z-9W armed variant was developed entirely in China. Pakistan operates the Z-9EC maritime variant.
famousMissions: PLA Navy South China Sea patrol

name: CH-148 Cyclone
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 composite blades) + tail rotor
description: The CH-148 Cyclone is the Royal Canadian Navy's replacement for the legendary Sea King, based on the Sikorsky S-92 with naval adaptations including folding blades and tail, full harpoon deck lock, and a comprehensive mission systems suite for ASW and ASuW operations.
historicalBackground: Canada's maritime helicopter replacement program is one of the most troubled in military aviation history. A 1992 contract was cancelled by the Chrétien government in 1993. A new competition awarded in 2004 to Sikorsky saw continued delays and penalties. Initial deliveries came in 2015, over 20 years after the original requirement.
famousMissions: Royal Canadian Navy Atlantic ASW patrols
HMCS Halifax carrier task group deployment (2021)

name: H175 / EC175
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The H175 is a medium helicopter jointly developed by Airbus Helicopters and China's AVIC, positioned between the H155 and H225 in the offshore oil and gas transport market. It features retractable landing gear, full glass cockpit, and Category A single-engine performance.
historicalBackground: The EC175 program was launched in 2006 as a 50/50 joint venture between Eurocopter and China's AVIC. Its first flight came in 2009. The offshore market adoption has been slower than hoped due to the 2016 North Sea downturn and competition from the AW189. Norwegian operator Bristow was an early customer.

name: BK 117 / H145M
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The BK 117 is a joint German-Japanese twin-engine helicopter developed by MBB and Kawasaki, featuring a wide clamshell rear door ideal for medical stretcher loading. It has become a world-leading EMS helicopter in its H145M military guise with a five-blade composite rotor.
historicalBackground: The BK 117 program was announced in 1977 as MBB and Kawasaki's joint venture to produce a helicopter superior to their individual designs. The prototype first flew in 1979, and the type entered production in 1982. The design lineage continues in the modern H145 M with a five-blade shrouded rotor.

name: Sikorsky CH-54 Tarhe / Skycrane
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (6 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-64 Skycrane / CH-54 Tarhe is a dedicated flying crane helicopter with a bare-tube fuselage designed specifically for lifting massive external loads. It has no integral cargo hold — the payload hangs beneath. The commercial S-64 Air Crane is still used for firefighting and construction.
historicalBackground: Sikorsky developed the S-64 concept in the early 1960s for the US Army, which operated it as the CH-54 Tarhe in Vietnam. It retrieved over 380 downed aircraft worth $210 million during the war. Erickson Air-Crane bought the type certificate in 1992 and continues operating and manufacturing improved S-64 Air Cranes commercially.

name: Westland Wessex
engineType: Single or twin turboshaft (Rolls-Royce Gnome or Napier Gazelle)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades, foldable) + tail rotor
description: The Wessex was Britain's turbine-powered licence-built derivative of the Sikorsky S-58, with Westland replacing the piston engine with a turbine powerplant — an innovation that defined British helicopter development philosophy. It served the Royal Navy and RAF from 1960 to 2003.
historicalBackground: Westland signed a licence agreement with Sikorsky in 1956 and converted the S-58 to turbine power in partnership with Napier. The twin-engine HAS 3 entered Royal Navy service in 1967. The Wessex famously rescued survivors from the SS Atlantic Conveyor during the Falklands War, and carried Prince Charles and Princess Diana on their honeymoon.
famousMissions: Falklands War – Royal Navy ASW and transport (1982)

name: Westland Sea King
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades, foldable) + tail rotor
description: The Westland Sea King is Britain's licence-built version of the Sikorsky SH-3, extensively modified with British Rolls-Royce engines and mission equipment. It served as the Royal Navy's primary ASW helicopter for 40 years and the RAF's search and rescue helicopter until 2016.
historicalBackground: Westland obtained a Sikorsky Sea King licence in 1966 and significantly redesigned the aircraft with British engines and systems. The RAF's SAR Sea Kings became iconic, routinely rescuing sailors and mountain climbers. India, Germany, Australia, Pakistan, Belgium, and Egypt also flew the type. The last British SAR Sea Kings were retired in 2016.
famousMissions: North Sea Piper Alpha disaster rescue (1988)

name: PZL Mi-2 Hoplite
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-2 is a Soviet-designed twin-turbine light helicopter built entirely in Poland by PZL Świdnik under a unique arrangement where the USSR designed it but licensed 100% of production to Poland. Over 5,400 were built, equipping Warsaw Pact air forces in utility, trainer, and armed gunship roles.
historicalBackground: Mil designed the Mi-2 in the early 1960s as a turbine successor to the Mi-1, but uniquely assigned all production to Poland's PZL Świdnik factory in 1964 — the only case of a Soviet helicopter being manufactured entirely outside the USSR. Production ran from 1965 to 1992. Poland still operates 50+.

name: Bell AH-1 Cobra
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (2 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AH-1 HueyCobra was the world's first purpose-designed attack helicopter, developed in crisis response to the Vietnam War's demand for armed escort. Its narrow fuselage, stub wings, and tandem cockpit established the template for all subsequent attack helicopters worldwide.
historicalBackground: Bell developed the Cobra in just six months in 1965 by mating the UH-1 Huey's proven rotor, engine, and transmission with a new narrow fuselage. It entered Vietnam service in 1967 and transformed helicopter warfare. The twin-engine AH-1J SeaCobra served the Marines, evolving into today's AH-1Z Viper.
famousMissions: Vietnam War – armed escort and fire support (1967–1972)

name: Bell 212 / UH-1N Twin Huey
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6T-3B Twin Pac)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (2 blades, semi-rigid) + tail rotor
description: The Bell 212 Twin Huey is the twin-engine evolution of the ubiquitous Huey, powered by a coupled Pratt & Whitney Canada PT6T Twin Pac engine. It serves military, police, EMS, and offshore operators, and remains in production in Italy as the AB212 ASW for naval roles.
historicalBackground: Developed to meet Canadian Forces requirements for a twin-engine Huey, the 212 first flew in 1969 and entered service in 1971. Italy's Agusta built it under licence as the AB212, and in the naval ASW version with torpedo capability, it serves with 14 navies. The USMC operated the UH-1N until 2014.
famousMissions: US Marine Corps Pacific fleet operations

name: Sikorsky S-70i Black Hawk (international)
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The S-70i is the internationally produced Black Hawk, assembled at PZL Mielec in Poland for export customers. It is functionally identical to the US Army UH-60M but assembled in Europe to meet offset and domestic content requirements of European and Asian buyers.
historicalBackground: Lockheed Martin acquired Sikorsky in 2015, and through PZL Mielec (owned by Sikorsky since 2007) began producing Black Hawks for NATO allies. Poland ordered 22 for its Army, Thailand ordered 6, and Saudi Arabia and Philippines also placed orders. The arrangement allows European content to satisfy procurement rules.

name: AW189
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW189 is a large twin-engine helicopter designed for long-range offshore oil and gas operations and search and rescue. Its modern glass cockpit, health monitoring systems, and superior range capability make it a strong competitor to the Sikorsky S-92 in the offshore segment.
historicalBackground: Launched at the 2010 Farnborough Airshow, the AW189 filled a gap in Leonardo's lineup between the medium AW139 and the heavy AW101. It first flew in 2011 and entered service in 2014. The type is operated by major offshore operators including CHC, Bristow, and Era in the North Sea and Gulf of Mexico.
famousMissions: North Sea offshore oil support (UK, Norway)

name: Bell 412EP
engineType: Twin turboshaft (Pratt & Whitney PT6T-3D Twin Pac)
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade teetering rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 412 is the twin-engine evolution of the Bell 212, featuring a composite four-blade rotor replacing the original two-blade teetering design. It is widely used by military, police, EMS, and offshore operators around the world, valued for its reliability and ease of maintenance.
historicalBackground: Developed as an upgrade to the Bell 212, the 412 first flew in 1979 and entered production in 1981. It has been license-built in Italy (as the Agusta-Bell AB412) and Indonesia. The US Army flies a variant as the UH-1N Twin Huey. The 412EPI with advanced rotors entered service in 2013.
famousMissions: Philippine Air Force transport and HADR

name: Mi-28 Havoc
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-28 Havoc is Russia's dedicated attack helicopter and the Ka-52's tandem in the Russian Army Aviation. Armed with a 30mm 2A42 cannon and Ataka-V anti-tank missiles, its tandem cockpit and armored fuselage give crews exceptional survivability in combat.
historicalBackground: Developed as a rival to the Ka-50 in the 1980s, the Mi-28 first flew in November 1982 but lost the Soviet competition to the Ka-50. After the Soviet collapse, the Mi-28N night-capable variant was selected for Russian service alongside the Ka-52. It entered service in 2009 and saw combat in Syria from 2015.

name: EC120 Colibri
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + Fenestron
description: The EC120 Colibri (Hummingbird) is a five-seat single-engine light helicopter jointly developed by Eurocopter, CATIC of China, and Singapore's ST Aerospace. Its Fenestron tail and lightweight composite airframe make it an ideal primary training and corporate transport helicopter.
historicalBackground: A unique tri-national development launched in the 1990s, the EC120 first flew in 1995 as Eurocopter's entry into the competitive light turbine helicopter market. China's involvement in the program reflected a new era of Sino-European aerospace cooperation. Over 700 were built before production ended.

name: H130 / EC130
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + Fenestron
description: The H130 is a single-engine light helicopter derived from the AS350 Écureuil with an enlarged, Fenestron-equipped tail. It dominates the helicopter tourism market worldwide, particularly at the Grand Canyon and in Hawaii, thanks to its wide cabin with panoramic windows and very low noise signature.
historicalBackground: Developed as the EC130B4 and later rebranded H130, this helicopter first flew in 1999. Its spacious six-passenger cabin and Fenestron made it the preferred aircraft for tourist operators across the US, New Zealand, and South Africa. Blue Hawaiian Helicopters operates the world's largest fleet.
famousMissions: Kruger National Park game surveys (South Africa)

name: NH90 NFH
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades, foldable) + tail rotor
description: The NH90 NFH (NATO Frigate Helicopter) is the naval variant of the NH90, equipped with dipping sonar, sonobuoys, torpedoes, and anti-ship missiles for maritime operations. It replaces the Lynx and Sea King in multiple European navies.
historicalBackground: The NFH variant was developed alongside the TTH (tactical transport) version from the outset of the program. The Netherlands was first to declare the NFH operational in 2010. The German Navy operates it as the MK88A Sea Lion. France uses it from the Charles de Gaulle carrier group.
famousMissions: French Navy carrier operations – Charles de Gaulle
Netherlands Navy counter-piracy – Gulf of Aden

name: Sikorsky SH-3 Sea King
rotorConfig: Single main rotor + tail rotor
description: The SH-3 Sea King was the US Navy's premier anti-submarine helicopter for three decades and served as Marine One for US Presidents from Kennedy to Clinton. Its amphibious boat-hull hull design allows water landings, and it rescued Apollo astronauts from the ocean after splashdown.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1959 as the XHSS-2, the Sea King entered Navy service in 1961. It was license-built by Agusta (Italy), Mitsubishi (Japan), and Westland (UK) in large numbers. The UK Westland Sea King served as an ASW and SAR helicopter until 2016, a 55-year service life.
famousMissions: Falklands War – UK Westland Sea Kings (1982)

name: AW109 LUH
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW109 LUH (Light Utility Helicopter) is the South African Air Force's primary utility helicopter, selected in 2006 to replace the Alouette III after 40 years of service. It performs casualty evacuation, troop transport, VIP, and border surveillance duties.
historicalBackground: South Africa's Project Lapfundi selected the AW109 LUH in 2006, with 30 aircraft ordered. Denel Aviation assembles the aircraft locally under license, providing technology transfer and local industry participation. The SAAF began taking deliveries in 2011. The type replaced one of the longest-serving fleets in African aviation.
famousMissions: South African border surveillance – Mozambique border

name: CH-47F Chinook (HC6)
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The CH-47F is the definitive modern Chinook variant with a digital advanced flight control system (DAFCS), common avionics architecture system (CAAS), and improved vibration suppression. It entered US Army service in 2007 and is the standard for new-build and remanufactured Chinooks worldwide.
historicalBackground: Building on six decades of continuous production, the CH-47F represents a complete digital modernization of the Chinook airframe. The RAF designates their aircraft HC6 after a major upgrade programme. The F model's DAFCS significantly reduces pilot workload, especially during slung load and high-altitude operations.

name: Schweizer 300C (TH-300C)
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Schweizer 300 (originally Hughes 269) is one of the longest-serving light piston helicopters ever produced, used worldwide for primary flight training, agricultural spraying, and police patrol. Its simplicity and low cost have kept it in production for over six decades.
historicalBackground: Designed by Hughes Tool Company and first flown in 1956, the 269 was purchased by Schweizer in 1983 who continued production as the 300. The US Army flew it as the TH-55 Osage primary trainer during the Vietnam era. The type transitioned to the Schweizer 330 turbine variant.
famousMissions: US Army helicopter pilot training – Vietnam era (TH-55)
Law enforcement patrol – multiple US agencies

name: Mil Mi-2 Hoplite
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (3 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-2 Hoplite is a compact twin-engine light helicopter originally designed in the USSR but entirely manufactured in Poland by PZL Świdnik — the only Soviet-designed helicopter ever license-built exclusively outside the USSR. Over 5,400 were produced, serving across the Warsaw Pact and beyond.
historicalBackground: Designed by Mil in the early 1960s as a turbine replacement for the Mi-1, the Mi-2 first flew in September 1961. Soviet leadership decided to transfer all production to Poland's PZL Świdnik, which produced all 5,400+ Mi-2s from 1965 to 1992. An armed anti-tank variant carried AT-3 Sagger missiles.
famousMissions: Warsaw Pact military exercises – Cold War
Polish Army training and utility operations

name: Boeing CH-46 Sea Knight
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The CH-46 Sea Knight was the US Marine Corps' principal medium-lift helicopter for 40 years, ferrying troops from ship to shore in every major US amphibious operation from Vietnam to Iraq. Its tandem rotor design allowed shipboard operations from the assault ship's small deck spaces.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1958, the CH-46 entered Marine Corps service in 1964 and flew in Vietnam from 1966. Despite age and repeated crashes from dynamic rollover (rectified in upgrades), the Sea Knight remained in USMC service until 2015 when it was replaced by the MV-22 Osprey. Canada retired its CH-113 Labrador variant in 2004.
famousMissions: Vietnam War amphibious assaults and resupply (1966–1975)

name: AW149
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AW149 is a medium twin-engine military helicopter positioned between the AW139 and AW101, designed for hot-and-high combat environments. It can be configured for troop transport, MEDEVAC, maritime patrol, or armed escort with door guns and rockets.
historicalBackground: Developed in the late 2000s as a military derivative of the AW139, the AW149 first flew in 2009. Poland selected the AW149 in 2023 as its new medium military helicopter, placing the largest contract for the type. Egypt had previously ordered the AW149 in 2015.
famousMissions: Royal Thai Air Force VIP and utility operations

name: AS565 Panther / Dauphin
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + Fenestron
description: The AS565 Panther is the military variant of the successful Dauphin family, widely used in naval anti-submarine warfare, anti-ship missile attack, SAR, and border patrol. The US Coast Guard flies the HH-65 Dolphin variant for short-range recovery missions.
historicalBackground: Developed from the SA365 Dauphin civil helicopter, the AS365 first flew in 1979. The US Coast Guard competition of 1979 selected it as the HH-65A Dolphin, though the type suffered early engine reliability problems. China obtained a license to build the Z-9 variant, which became their primary military helicopter family.
famousMissions: US Coast Guard search and rescue (HH-65 Dolphin)

name: Harbin Z-9
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + Fenestron
description: The Z-9 is China's primary multipurpose military helicopter, derived from the Aerospatiale SA365 Dauphin under license. The Z-9W armed variant and Z-9D naval variant serve across the PLA's three services. It became the foundation for China's helicopter industry and led to the larger Z-20.
historicalBackground: China imported the SA365N Dauphin technology in 1980, initially assembling aircraft from French kits before progressive indigenisation. The Z-9 first flew in Chinese hands in 1981. Later Z-9W variants carried HJ-8 anti-tank missiles, and the Z-9D carries torpedoes and dipping sonar from PLA Navy frigates.

name: NH90 TTH
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The NH90 TTH (Tactical Transport Helicopter) is the land-based variant of the NH90, used for troop insertion, CASEVAC, combat search and rescue, and special forces support. Its full fly-by-wire system and composite airframe are advanced for a military transport of its class.
historicalBackground: Germany was first to declare the TTH operational in 2007. The type has served in ISAF Afghanistan with German, Italian, and other NATO contingents. Finland's cancellation in 2022 citing performance shortfalls raised concerns across the program, though other operators remain satisfied.

name: Sikorsky S-300C
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-300C (formerly Schweizer 300C) is a piston-powered three-seat trainer widely used for private pilot training and low-cost utility work. It is simple, forgiving, and cost-effective — ideal for introducing student pilots to rotary-wing flight fundamentals.
historicalBackground: After acquiring Schweizer Aircraft in 2004, Sikorsky continued producing the 300C until 2012 when production was discontinued. The aircraft had been in production under various owners for nearly 50 years. The type is still widely flown by flight schools worldwide as a low-cost entry-level trainer.

name: HAL Light Combat Helicopter (LCH) Prachand
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 composite blades) + tail rotor
description: The HAL LCH Prachand (Fierce) is India's indigenously designed attack helicopter, optimized for high-altitude operations in the Himalayas. Armed with a 20mm turret gun, Dhruvastra anti-tank missiles, and air-to-air missiles, it is the world's lightest attack helicopter able to operate at altitudes above 5,000 metres.
historicalBackground: Initiated in response to the 1999 Kargil War where India lacked high-altitude attack helicopter capability, the LCH program formally started in 2006 and first flew in March 2010. After a decade-long test program, it entered Indian Army and Air Force service in 2022, named Prachand. It is India's first indigenously designed combat helicopter.
famousMissions: Indian Army Siachen Glacier high-altitude patrol

name: Bell 505 Jet Ranger X
rotorConfig: Two-blade semi-rigid main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 505 is Bell's modern entry-level single-turbine helicopter, positioned as the successor to the Bell 206 JetRanger in the training and light utility market. With a full glass Garmin G1000H cockpit, it provides turbine performance at near-piston costs.
historicalBackground: Developed in the 2010s with significant input from the training and light utility markets, the Bell 505 first flew in 2014 and entered production in Mirabel, Canada in 2016. China's CASC has a significant sales agreement. Thailand selected it for military pilot training, and it is widely used across Asia and the Americas.
famousMissions: Royal Thai Air Force pilot training
South Korean civil pilot training programs

name: Mi-38
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (6 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-38 is Russia's newest medium helicopter, designed to replace the Mi-8/Mi-17 in offshore and executive transport roles. With a glass cockpit, Western-style redundant avionics, and significantly greater power than the Mi-8, it targets the international offshore and VIP market.
historicalBackground: Development began in the 1990s as a joint Russian-European project with Eurocopter involvement, which was later cancelled after political and commercial disagreements. Russia continued alone with the Klimov engine and first flew the prototype in 2003. Production began in 2016. Russian government and state corporations are its primary customers.

name: EC175 / H175
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The H175 is a medium twin-engine helicopter jointly developed by Airbus and China's AVIC, positioned between the H155 and H225. It features the latest composite airframe technology, Blue-Edge blade-slap-reducing rotor blades, and is targeted at offshore oil and gas transport and SAR.
historicalBackground: Announced in 2007 as the EC175 (later H175), this helicopter represents a significant Sino-French aerospace partnership, with AVIC's Harbin subsidiary producing major structural components. It first flew in 2009, was certified in 2014, and entered offshore service with Era Helicopters in the Gulf of Mexico.
famousMissions: Gulf of Mexico offshore oil operations

name: Sikorsky S-61L / S-61N
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-61 is the commercial airliner variant of the SH-3 Sea King, the first helicopter used on scheduled airline routes. Its amphibious boat hull and exceptional reliability made it the dominant offshore helicopter of the 1970s and 1980s in the North Sea.
historicalBackground: New York Airways began the world's first scheduled helicopter airline service with the S-61L in 1953. The S-61N amphibious variant became the workhorse of North Sea oil exploration in the 1970s. Though production ended in 1980, many S-61s remain flying in remote areas including Alaska and Greenland.
famousMissions: New York Airways scheduled airline service (1953–1979)

name: Piasecki H-21 Shawnee
rotorConfig: Tandem rotor
description: The H-21 Shawnee, nicknamed the 'Flying Banana' for its distinctive curved tandem rotor fuselage, was the US Army's primary troop transport helicopter in the early Vietnam War era. It was the first US Army helicopter deployed to Vietnam in significant numbers.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1952, the H-21 was the first true troop transport helicopter in US service. In 1957 a modified H-21 made the first non-stop helicopter crossing of the continental United States. In Vietnam from 1962, the H-21 conducted early air assault operations before being replaced by the UH-1 Huey.
famousMissions: First helicopter crossing of US continent (1957)

name: Bell 429 GlobalRanger
rotorConfig: Four-blade semi-rigid rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 429 is Bell's flagship light twin helicopter, featuring a full glass Garmin G2000H cockpit, health and usage monitoring systems, and an exceptionally wide flat-floor cabin. It excels in EMS operations thanks to its spacious interior and wide clamshell doors.
historicalBackground: Developed to meet growing demand for a modern, safe light twin, the Bell 429 first flew in 2007 and was certified in 2009. South Korea chose it as the basis for the LCH (Law Enforcement Helicopter) with domestic sensors. Thailand, Kuwait, and the UAE operate it for VIP transport and police roles.
famousMissions: Korean National Police aviation unit operations

name: Bell 222
rotorConfig: Two-blade semi-rigid rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 222 was Bell's first light twin-engine helicopter, featuring retractable landing gear and an executive-class interior. It gained worldwide pop-culture fame as the fictional 'Airwolf' in the 1980s TV series. A reliable EMS and VIP helicopter, it was in production from 1980 to 1989.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1976, the Bell 222 represented Bell's entry into the competitive light twin market alongside the Agusta A109. It entered service in 1980 and served many US hospitals as an EMS helicopter throughout the 1980s. The Bell 222UT (utility twin) with skids instead of retractable gear was more popular commercially.
famousMissions: New York City EMS helicopter operations (1980s)

name: H145M (EC645 T2)
rotorConfig: Four-blade bearingless main rotor + Fenestron
description: The H145M is the military version of the H145, used for special operations, MEDEVAC, light attack, and reconnaissance. Germany's KSK special forces, Luxembourg, and Serbia operate it. It can be fitted with a chin-mounted gun, door guns, rockets, or surveillance systems.
historicalBackground: Selected by multiple NATO nations for special operations support, the H145M entered German military service in 2015. Hungary ordered 20 in 2018, becoming the largest customer. The five-blade H145M variant, introduced in 2020 with a bearingless rotor, offers improved performance and lower vibration.
famousMissions: German KSK special forces support – Afghanistan and Mali

name: Kamov Ka-62
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + shrouded tail rotor
description: The Ka-62 is Kamov's first helicopter with a conventional single-main-rotor layout rather than the trademark coaxial design. Targeting the international medium utility and offshore market, it was designed with Western components, though sanctions following 2022 have forced substitution with Russian alternatives.
historicalBackground: Kamov began the Ka-62 to compete with the AW139 and H175 in the commercial medium market using Western engines and avionics. First flown in 2016, certification delays and then Western component sanctions following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 severely disrupted the program, with full Russian-component variants still in development.

name: Bell-Boeing V-22 Osprey
rotorConfig: Tiltrotor (2 × proprotors, 38 ft diameter each)
description: The V-22 Osprey is a tiltrotor aircraft combining helicopter vertical lift with turboprop fixed-wing cruise speed and range. It operates at nearly twice the speed and altitude of conventional helicopters, enabling the USMC and SOCOM to conduct long-range assault and special operations missions previously impossible.
historicalBackground: A revolutionary concept from the 1980s, the V-22 suffered three fatal crashes during development and was nearly cancelled. After extensive redesign it entered USMC service as the MV-22B in 2007 and deployed to Iraq. The USAF CV-22B serves SOCOM. Despite a high accident rate (including 2022 and 2023 crashes), it remains irreplaceable for its unique performance.

name: Westland Sea King HAS6
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades) + tail rotor
