description: The Westland Sea King is a license-built version of the Sikorsky SH-3, developed and improved by Westland with Rolls-Royce engines and British systems. It served the Royal Navy and RAF in SAR and ASW roles for 47 years, flying in the Falklands, Gulf War, and numerous SAR missions around the UK coast.
historicalBackground: Westland built the Sea King under license from Sikorsky, first flying the British version in 1969. The Royal Navy's SAR Sea Kings became icons of UK search and rescue along the coastline. Prince William flew the Sea King as an RAF SAR pilot in Wales. The last UK Sea King retired in 2016 after 47 years of service.

name: Sikorsky S-58 / H-34 Choctaw
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Sikorsky S-58 (US military H-34 Choctaw) was the last large piston-powered helicopter in widespread military use, bridging the piston and turbine eras. It served in Korea aftermath, Vietnam, and with a dozen nations. Licensed to Sud Aviation (France) and Westland (UK), it was built in three countries.
historicalBackground: First flown in March 1954, the H-34 served across all US military branches. The USMC used it extensively in early Vietnam operations. France used the H-34/HSS in Algeria and Indochina. Sud Aviation produced over 160 under license. Many were later re-engined with turbines, dramatically extending their working lives.
famousMissions: USMC Leatherneck landing on USS Wasp exercises

name: Mil Mi-4 Hound
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The Mi-4 Hound was the Soviet Union's answer to the Sikorsky H-19, which Stalin ordered developed at emergency speed after seeing it demonstrated. The Mi-4 became the backbone of Soviet transport aviation in the 1950s–60s, serving in 35+ countries and license-built in China as the Harbin Z-5.
historicalBackground: Developed in record time (just over a year) on Stalin's direct order after he was shown American helicopter capabilities in Korea, the Mi-4 first flew in May 1952. Over 3,000 were built, and China produced an additional 545 as the Z-5. It was operated by nearly every Soviet bloc nation and many non-aligned states.
famousMissions: Cuban Missile Crisis Soviet forces Cuba
Harbin Z-5 Korean People's Army service

name: EC155 / H155 Dauphin
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (5 blades, Spheriflex) + Fenestron
description: The EC155/H155 is the top-of-the-range Dauphin, featuring a Spheriflex composite rotor hub, Fenestron tail, and expansive flat-floor cabin. With a maximum speed approaching 324 km/h it is among the fastest production helicopters, widely used for corporate VIP, police, and coast guard duties.
historicalBackground: Launched as the EC155B at the 1997 Paris Airshow, this helicopter evolved from the SA365 Dauphin lineage. The French Gendarmerie operates it for police and mountain rescue. South Korea's coast guard flies it on offshore patrol. The H155 rebrand followed Airbus Helicopters' 2014 product naming overhaul.
famousMissions: Executive VIP transport across the Middle East

name: Kaman HH-43 Huskie
rotorConfig: Intermeshing counter-rotating rotors (synchropter)
description: The Kaman HH-43 Huskie was the USAF's standard airfield crash rescue and local base rescue helicopter throughout the 1960s. Its intermeshing rotor system (synchropter) allowed it to hover with remarkable stability near fires. In Vietnam it flew over 900 combat rescues.
historicalBackground: Kaman's expertise in intermeshing rotors produced the HH-43 as the Air Force's crash rescue helicopter at every major USAF base during the Cold War era. In Vietnam, detachments called Detachment 'Kaman Rescue' operated at bases in South Vietnam and Thailand, earning numerous combat rescue credits before the HH-3 took over.
famousMissions: USAF airfield crash rescue – worldwide Cold War bases

name: Agusta A109A
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid four-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Agusta A109A was Italy's first homegrown twin-engine helicopter and established Agusta as a world-class manufacturer. Sleek and fast, the A109 has been used by the Argentine Navy in the Falklands War, the Belgian Army as a scout, and by law enforcement agencies worldwide.
historicalBackground: First flown in August 1971, the A109 represented a major technological leap for Italian industry. The Argentine Navy used A109As to fly anti-submarine patrols during the Falklands War, and one was reportedly used in covert operations. The A109K2 high-altitude variant with Arriel engines was developed for Swiss Alpine rescue.
famousMissions: Argentine Navy – Falklands War anti-submarine patrols (1982)
Belgian Army scout operations – Cold War and Bosnia

name: Robinson R66 Turbine
rotorConfig: Semi-rigid two-blade main rotor + tail rotor
description: The Robinson R66 Turbine is Robinson's first turbine-powered helicopter, extending the R44 design with a Rolls-Royce RR300 engine that delivers better high-altitude and hot-day performance. It seats five and costs a fraction of competitive five-seat turbines from Airbus or Bell.
historicalBackground: Frank Robinson developed the R66 in his 80s, first flying it in 2007 and certifying it in 2010. The Rolls-Royce engine partnership was crucial for certification. The R66 quickly became the best-selling five-seat turbine helicopter, attracting buyers from helicopter tour operators, private owners, and first-time turbine operators.
famousMissions: Grand Canyon and Hawaiian scenic tour operations

name: AS332 Super Puma
rotorConfig: Single main rotor (4 blades) + tail rotor
description: The AS332 Super Puma is a major upgrade of the SA330 Puma with more powerful engines, composite rotor blades, and extended fuselage. It became the dominant North Sea offshore helicopter in the 1980s–1990s and spawned the EC225/H225 family. The Swiss Air Force uses it for alpine troop transport.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1978, the Super Puma entered service in 1981. The civil AS332L1 variant with 17-passenger cabin became the North Sea standard. The Swiss Air Force Super Puma is specifically designed for high-altitude alpine operations. The Indonesian Air Force flies the AS332 for VIP transport of the President.
famousMissions: Swiss Army alpine troop transport – Alps

name: Bell 407
rotorConfig: Four-blade semi-rigid rotor + tail rotor
description: The Bell 407 is a single-turbine utility helicopter derived from the OH-58D Kiowa Warrior with an improved four-blade composite rotor and wide-body cabin. It is widely used for EMS, law enforcement, tour operations, and executive transport. The 407GXi introduced an all-glass Garmin cockpit.
historicalBackground: First flown in 1994, the Bell 407 was developed from the 206L LongRanger with lessons from the OH-58D program. It entered service in 1996 and was selected as the platform for the Northrop Grumman MQ-8B Fire Scout unmanned helicopter. The 407GXP introduced improved performance with the FADEC-controlled engine.
famousMissions: US Customs and Border Protection surveillance

name: Kamov Ka-32
rotorConfig: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors
payload: 5,000 kg (11,023 lb) slung or 3,700 kg internal
description: The Kamov Ka-32 is the civilian and export variant of the Ka-27 naval helicopter, used worldwide for aerial firefighting, construction crane operations, maritime SAR, and cargo transport. Its coaxial rotor eliminates the tail rotor, reducing downwash area and improving stability during precision sling loads.
historicalBackground: Developed alongside the naval Ka-27, the Ka-32 first flew in 1980 and entered commercial use in the late 1980s. The Ka-32A11BC firefighting variant, certified in Europe, became popular with Portuguese, Spanish, and South Korean fire services. The type is notable for operating in built-up urban environments due to its compact footprint.
famousMissions: Portuguese aerial firefighting operations – Pedrógão Grande fire (2017)
